summary: Gruber pushes back against breathless reports of 'panic' at Apple over the upcoming iPhone's development, arguing that what's being reported as crisis is simply the normal summer crunch cycle for iOS engineers. He acknowledges that inductive charging software may not be ready for the initial launch, but frames this as a minor disappointment comparable to the delayed Portrait Mode on iPhone 7 Plus. He's particularly skeptical of claims that Apple is still making hardware decisions in July, noting that production tooling would already be underway for a September launch. The piece also addresses the Touch ID removal rumor, suggesting that if true, Apple's replacement face-scanning technology would need to be equally good or better.
key_points: Reports of 'panic' at Apple are the predictable annual cycle of iPhone rumor season — features get hyped, then reported as delayed or troubled
Inductive charging may ship late (with iOS 11.1), but this mirrors the Portrait Mode delay and isn't cause for alarm
Hardware decisions for this year's iPhones were made months ago — by July, Apple is in validation testing, not designing
Current rumors are 'echoes of decisions that have already been made,' not real-time reporting on active deliberations
If the OLED iPhone ships without Touch ID, the replacement face scanner must be equally fast, accurate, and trustworthy — anything less would be a serious warning sign
money_quotes: "Apple engineers are panicking" is an exciting story. "Apple engineers are in crunch mode to finish iOS 11.0 just like they are every summer" is not.
I believe Apple is months ahead of these rumors — what we hear now with reports like this are just echoes of decisions that have already been made.
But I think it's bonkers to think that Apple is still working on hardware decisions like this in the middle of July.
With software Apple can (and does) play a bit fast and loose. iOS 11.0 won't be baked until late August. But software can (and always is) patched. Hardware doesn't work like that.
Many of the decisions related to the hardware on this year's new iPhones were made two years ago.
If the hardware were still up in the air today, it'd be an impossible target for the software, never mind for Apple's incredibly high-scale production ramp.
If the new iPhone ships without a Touch ID sensor and there is no replacement authentication technology that is as good or better than Touch ID — that would be a dead canary in the coal mine.
key_insight: The annual iPhone rumor cycle mistakes normal engineering crunch for crisis, and reporters claiming Apple is still deciding hardware features in July fundamentally misunderstand how hardware product development works at scale.
